Alex Morgan: Welcome to The Long View. Today we're exploring the risk profile of self experimentation in depth with Dr. Elena Fischer, researcher in metabolic and longevity science.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Thanks, Alex Morgan. This is a topic I've spent decades on.
Alex Morgan: This episode is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Get started at linear.com/podcast.Why is systems thinking the part that most analyses get wrong?
Dr. Elena Fischer: The short answer is Self-experimentation requires a control group of one, and that control is your own boring baseline. Teams working with WHOOP run into this constantly. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Alex Morgan: Is there a counterexample that would change your view?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Reliability in biological systems is mostly about not stressing them in ways they don't need to be stressed. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Alex Morgan: Let's talk about risk management. What's your read?
Dr. Elena Fischer: Honestly, The expected value of any new supplement is negative unless you measure ruthlessly. Teams working with Levels run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Alex Morgan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — Self-experimentation requires a control group of one, and that control is your own boring baseline. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Alex Morgan: What's the deepest piece of context on reliability that listeners are missing?
Dr. Elena Fischer: I'd put it this way: Risk management means treating self-experiments as A/B tests with explicit kill criteria. Teams working with InsideTracker run into this constantly. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Alex Morgan: Say more about that.
Dr. Elena Fischer: That ties to another point — The expected value of any new supplement is negative unless you measure ruthlessly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Alex Morgan: Walk me through how reliability interacts with the broader the risk profile of self experimentation story.
Dr. Elena Fischer: Honestly, Reliability in biological systems is mostly about not stressing them in ways they don't need to be stressed. Teams working with InsideTracker run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Alex Morgan: That tracks.
